data.title: A 21-Year-Old Focused On Building His Credit Score Ended Up $25K In Debt. 'We Played With The Snakes, We Got Bit,' Says 'Ramsey Show' Host
data.description:
A 21-year-old from South Carolina thought he was doing the right thing by focusing on his credit score after high school. Instead, that decision resulted in $25,000 of debt and a hard lesson about how quickly things can spiral when income drops.

Speaking on “The Ramsey Show” recently, Nehemiah explained that he had built up credit quickly while working, only to run into trouble after losing his job and taking a lower-paying role. “I thought credit was the main thing that I had to focus on,” he said. “I ran it up pretty quick.”

Don't Miss:

Why Credit Scores Can Be Misleading

“We tried that game,” co-host George Kamel told Nehemiah, using the moment to challenge the idea that a strong credit score equals financial health. “We played with the snakes. We got bit.”

Kamel added that credit scores are designed for one purpose: “To get you some more debt at better rates.” In his view, chasing a higher score often results in more borrowing, not more wealth. “That’s the game.”

Trending: Skip the Regrets: The Essential Retirement Tips Experts Wish Everyone Knew Earlier.

A Plan To Get Out Of Debt Faster

Kamel broke the situation down into simple math. With $25,000 in debt, paying $1,000 a month would take about two years to become debt-free. “So the goal is how do we get out of debt in less than two years?” he said.

That means increasing income and reducing expenses to create what he called “margin,” or extra money that can be thrown at the debt.

Co-host Rachel Cruze pushed the idea further, suggesting the caller take on additional work. “Could you make it a goal to earn an extra $1,000 a month by working nights and weekends and just working like crazy?” she asked.

If he could reach that level, his total monthly payments could jump to $1,600, cutting the payoff timeline closer to 15 to 18 months.

Both hosts acknowledged the added pressure of helping his mother, who is dealing with medical issues, but pointed out that her eventual return to work could free up more cash flow.

Cruze emphasized that the caller's age is his biggest advantage. Learning these lessons at 21 could dramatically change his financial future.

She laid out a long-term plan: first eliminate the debt, then build a three-month emergency fund, and finally begin investing. If he starts investing $1,000 a month at age 25 and earns an average 10% return, that could grow to about $6.3 million by retirement.

Nehemiah's story highlights how easy it is to get caught up in credit scores while neglecting the bigger picture. Platforms like Money Pickle give users a clear view of their finances, helping them manage debt, plan spending, and build a roadmap for long-term goals—so young adults can learn from mistakes early and make smarter financial decisions.

Image: Shutterstock

data.description:

data.title: Why Polymarket's $20 Billion Valuation Ask Might Actually Be Conservative.
data.description:
Kalshi just raised at an 83.5x fee multiple.

If you apply even a fraction of that to Polymarket’s projected fees, the platform’s reported $20 billion valuation target starts to look like a discount.

A new valuation report from Messari researcher 0xWeiler builds the case from the ground up.

Kalshi raised $1 billion at a $22 billion valuation on March 19, led by Coatue Management. With $263.5 million in 2025 fees, that establishes the 83.5x benchmark.

0xWeiler’s base case for Polymarket uses a 30x multiple on 2028 projected fees, well below Kalshi’s current figure.

The model benchmarks against Kalshi, DraftKings (NASDAQ:DKNG), and traditional exchange infrastructure, then discounts for fee multiple compression as the revenue base grows.

The Volume Behind The Math

Prediction markets processed $23.2 billion in February 2026, up over 1,200% year-over-year. Polymarket captured roughly a third of that, with sports, crypto, and politics each driving meaningful share.

The report projects volume growth through 2028 using back-to-back NFL season comparisons as the baseline. The base case models 50% annual growth, with a bear at 30% and bull at 100%. Even the bear case implies a sector that dwarfs what existed 18 months ago.

On March 30, Polymarket expands taker fees across nearly all categories for the first time. Crypto peaks at 1.80%, politics at 1.00%. Only geopolitics stays free. With $9.55 billion in recent 30-day volume, annualized fee revenue may approach $300 million.

What Could Go Wrong

A POLY token and airdrop have been confirmed by Polymarket’s team.

If the token launches at or near the $20 billion figure, early users who farmed volume on the platform could see significant payouts.

That also introduces a risk: much of Polymarket’s current volume may be airdrop-driven, which could evaporate once the incentive disappears.

Other risks include competition from Kalshi, Hyperliquid, and Robinhood, and increased legal pressure.

Image: Shutterstock
